content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

July 2, 1991

Anthony Saul Alperin
Assistant City Attorney
City of Los Angeles
1800 City Hall East
Los Angeles, CA  90012

Re:  Your Request for Informal Assistance
     Our File No. I-91-324

Dear Mr. Alperin:

     This letter is in response to your letter of June 27, 1991,
in which you expressed concern that the ordinance established by
the City of Los Angeles to require developers to pay the costs
incurred by the city with respect to developers' land use
applications may result in the disqualification of contractors
paid from those funds.  Specifically, you were concerned that
contractors who are hired by the city to work on
developers' land use applications are compensated by the city from
fees or other such payments paid to the city by developers, the
developers would be considered sources of income to these city
contractors.  Apparently, your concern arose from your
interpretation of the advice contained in the Savage Advice
Letter, No. I-90-622a (copy enclosed.)

     Based on the ordinance in question and other city procedures
related to the ordinance, it appears as though the system in place
in the City of Los Angeles does not create a source of income
problem.  Though Ordinance No. 166,859 is silent in certain areas
or else does not address with enough specificity some of our
concerns, you have represented to staff, both in your letter and
during subsequent telephone conversations on June 28, 1991 and
July 1, 1991, that the ordinance along with other city procedures

_________________________
1  Ordinance No. 166,859, Los Angeles Administrative Code,
   §§ 5.400 et seq.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-91-324
Page 2


related to the ordinance satisfy the criteria set forth below.2
Assuming, therefore, that these criteria are met, we conclude that
the system currently in place in the City of Los Angeles does not
create a situation in which developers who pay the costs assessed
by the city for processing land use applications are sources of
income to city contractors paid from those funds as a result of
those payments:

     (1)  The city has sole discretion to select which of its
employees are assigned to work on the developer's land use
application.

     (2)  The city has sole discretion to determine which persons
the city will hire as employees and contractors to work on the
developer's land use application.  (In this regard, we understand
the city has in place a formalized procedure ("a RFP") which it
utilizes to identify and select persons with whom it enters into
contracts.)

     (3)  The city has sole discretion to direct the work and
evaluate the performance of the employees and contractors whom the
city hires to work on the developer's land use application.  This
includes the right to fire or replace at any time any employee or
contractor who is assigned to work on the developer's land use
application.

     (4)  The city has the sole discretion to determine the amount
of compensation paid to employees or contractors who are hired by
the city to work on the developer's land use application.

     (5)  The city, not the developer, pays the employee or
contractor hired or assigned by the city to work on the
developer's land use application from a city account under the
exclusive control of the city.

     (6)  The written agreement entered into between the city and
the contractor who is hired to work on the developer's land use
application is separate from the agreement between the city and
the developer.  The agreement with the contractor specifies that
the terms of the contractor's compensation is not dependent upon
the city's approval of the developer's project.

     (7)  The written agreement entered into between the city and
the developer concerning the developer's land use application,


_______________________________
     2  Please note that this letter is based on the facts presented to
us.  The Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Ogelsby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71, copy enclosed.)

Our File No. I-91-324
Page 3


including the terms of the payments to the city, is formalized by
resolution or written public record of the city. This agreement
specifies that processing the developer's land use application is
not contingent on the hiring of any specific contractor.

A more detailed letter containing the legal analysis for our
determination herein will follow under separate cover.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin,
Acting General Counsel

By: Deanne Stone
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:DS:aa

Enclosures

sections.conclusion: Assuming the criteria are met, we conclude that the system currently in place in the city of Los Angeles does not create a situation in which developers who pay the costs assessed by the city for processing land use applications are sources of income to city contractors paid from those funds as a result of those payments.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether city contractors hired to work on developers' land use applications who are compensated from developer fees paid to the city would be disqualified due to the developers being considered sources of income.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that the Los Angeles ordinance establishing developer-funded cost recovery for land use applications does not create a source of income problem for city contractors, provided that the city maintains sole discretion over contractor selection, compensation, and performance oversight, and that contractor agreements are separate from developer agreements.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: No explicit QUESTION or CONCLUSION headers, but structure follows standard advice letter format with question embedded in initial paragraph and conclusion starting at 'Based on the ordinance.' Letter references Savage Advice Letter No. I-90-622a. Document indicates a more detailed letter with legal analysis would follow separately.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether city contractors hired to work on developers' land use applications who are compensated from developer fees paid to the city would be disqualified due to the developers being considered sources of income.

CONCLUSION: Assuming the criteria are met, we conclude that the system currently in place in the city of Los Angeles does not create a situation in which developers who pay the costs assessed by the city for processing land use applications are sources of income to city contractors paid from those funds as a result of those payments.

embedding.summary: The FPPC responded to the City of Los Angeles Assistant City Attorney regarding concerns that requiring developers to pay costs related to their land use applications could disqualify contractors compensated from those fees. The FPPC concluded no conflict exists if the city maintains sole control over contractor selection, compensation, and oversight.
